# Questions in Livestream 153

Live Stream #153: Philosophy Is A Walk On A Slippery Rock

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp. The links use HTML anchors so GitHub can render them with `target="_blank"`.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction |
|---:|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/B-mR9TACPCM?t=839" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">13:59</a> | What do you think of the argument from embarrassment? | He thinks it has real value, though not as a perfect stand-alone proof, because embarrassing details often mark less mythologized reporting. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/B-mR9TACPCM?t=1004" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">16:44</a> | Are `agape`, `phileo`, and `eros` clearly distinct in first-century Greek? | He says the distinctions had largely flattened by the first century, so the words can overlap heavily in practice. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/B-mR9TACPCM?t=1247" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">20:47</a> | Was Jesus' conversation with Peter about feeding the flock originally in Greek or Aramaic? | He says the transcript cannot establish which language was original; either Greek or Aramaic is possible in that setting. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/B-mR9TACPCM?t=1432" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">23:52</a> | In Leviticus 19:10, what nuance does the word for "sojourner" carry? | He says it refers to a protected non-Israelite sojourner, not merely a random stranger. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/B-mR9TACPCM?t=1661" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">27:41</a> | Is a sojourner more like a traveler or a wanderer? | He says a sojourner is more like a permitted wanderer living off the land than a passing traveler. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/B-mR9TACPCM?t=1693" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">28:13</a> | Could women foreigners take part in Passover? | Yes; he says women could participate, with a much lighter ritual requirement than circumcision. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/B-mR9TACPCM?t=1841" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">30:41</a> | When God "remembers" in passages like Genesis 9:15, is that anthropomorphic? | He says it is better understood as God deciding to act, not as God passively recalling forgotten information. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/B-mR9TACPCM?t=2072" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">34:32</a> | Did rich Egyptians live beside poor Egyptians, and did priests live in temples? | Priests did not live in temples, and housing could be socially stratified, with better and worse districts like later cities. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/B-mR9TACPCM?t=2505" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">41:45</a> | How should Genesis 6:6's language of God regretting or grieving be interpreted? | He frames it positionally: God reacts to iniquity with anger and grief while remaining unchanged in essence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/B-mR9TACPCM?t=3109" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">51:49</a> | How should the gift of prophecy be understood today? | He says prophecy is mostly about rightly interpreting the present, not mainly predicting the future. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/B-mR9TACPCM?t=3676" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:01:16</a> | Do 1 Corinthians 2:3-5 and 4:20 mean teachers cannot add anything of themselves? | He says Paul warns against faith resting on clever rhetoric rather than God's power, not against all teaching or explanation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/B-mR9TACPCM?t=4094" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:08:14</a> | What was the bigger contribution, Assassin's Creed Origins or the spam jerky? | He says Assassin's Creed Origins wins because it funds weeks of programming and equipment upgrades for future work. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/B-mR9TACPCM?t=4334" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:12:14</a> | Was the torn temple curtain about access to God's throne room, the Spirit going out, or both? | He says both: the sacred is opened to the world and believers gain access to the throne room. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/B-mR9TACPCM?t=4908" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:21:48</a> | Why is the Nile so dirty? | He says seasonal rains and runoff carry material down the Nile, a condition he treats as long-standing rather than modern only. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/B-mR9TACPCM?t=5473" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:31:13</a> | What do you think of the conflict thesis and Dawkins's recent "cultural Christian" comments? | He treats Dawkins's comments as inconsistent and says stripping away Christianity leaves a vacuum that should not surprise anyone when something else fills it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/B-mR9TACPCM?t=6516" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:48:36</a> | Why is the red heifer important? | He says the red heifer is required for temple-purification water and thus for inaugurating a functioning temple. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/B-mR9TACPCM?t=6687" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:51:27</a> | What does it mean to be human in Christianity? | He gives a brief Christian-anthropology answer: humans bear God's image and are meant for relationship with God. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/B-mR9TACPCM?t=6839" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:53:59</a> | What are the problems with skepticism overall? | He says skepticism becomes self-defeating when it is not skeptical of its own skepticism and narrows evidence too far. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/B-mR9TACPCM?t=6976" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:56:16</a> | What is your favorite argument for God's existence? | He says he does not have a favorite classical argument and instead leans evidentialist, following the preponderance of evidence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/B-mR9TACPCM?t=7592" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:06:32</a> | What do you think of reading Genesis 2 as a more detailed account of Genesis 6? | He says he no longer favors that reading and expects pushback when people first encounter toledot and consecutive-vav arguments. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/B-mR9TACPCM?t=8147" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:15:47</a> | How should readers approach the church fathers? | He says slowly and attentively, because their long-form arguments assume a level of retention and patience that modern readers often lack. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/B-mR9TACPCM?t=8549" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:22:29</a> | Did ancient Egyptian have regional variation? | He says probably yes, but the lack of vowels and limited Lower Egyptian material make the details hard to reconstruct. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/B-mR9TACPCM?t=9029" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:30:29</a> | What do you think of the Orthodox Jewish claim that both written and oral Torah were given at Sinai? | He accepts written Torah at Sinai but rejects the claim that the later oral Torah was given there. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/B-mR9TACPCM?t=9165" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:32:45</a> | How should readers compare fatherhood in 1 John 3:1 and brotherhood in Hebrews 2:1? | He points to adoption as God's children and co-heirship with Christ, while noting the question is too large for a full treatment. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/B-mR9TACPCM?t=9542" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:39:02</a> | Were Egyptians better at portraying animals than medieval European artists? | He says it depends on style, purpose, artist, and medium; material limitations matter more than a simple better-or-worse comparison. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/B-mR9TACPCM?t=10531" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:55:31</a> | What do you think about universalism, annihilationism, and eternal conscious torment, and which apologists do you recommend? | He says he holds passive eternal conscious torment, is unconvinced by annihilationism, and recommends several apologetics channels. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/B-mR9TACPCM?t=12127" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:22:07</a> | Is there a majestic plural case ending in Egyptian grammar? | He says not really; majestic plural is more characteristic of Semitic languages, though he leaves room for missed examples. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/B-mR9TACPCM?t=12241" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:24:01</a> | Are any Egyptian cognates plausible equivalents to the Hebrew Yahweh? | He says no clear Egyptian equivalent works, because Egyptian-Hebrew transliteration rules do not support the common proposals. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/B-mR9TACPCM?t=12730" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:32:10</a> | Why would Pharaoh expect Hebrew midwives to kill Israelite babies? | He says royal commands in Egypt were backed by force, so the expectation itself fits the culture even if the women bravely refused. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/B-mR9TACPCM?t=12595" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:29:55</a> | Were the midwives in Exodus 1 Egyptian or Israelite? | He says they were Israelite, because Shiphrah and Puah are Semitic rather than Egyptian names. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/B-mR9TACPCM?t=13158" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:39:18</a> | Did ancient Egyptians have anything equivalent to modern slang? | He says yes, late Egyptian functions as a street-language development from more formal Middle Egyptian. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/B-mR9TACPCM?t=13770" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:49:30</a> | Did Egyptian husbands and wives sleep in the same bed? | Yes, if the bed was large enough; he says double beds and shared sleeping arrangements are plausible in the evidence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/B-mR9TACPCM?t=13955" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:52:35</a> | Are the Twelve Prophets one book or twelve separate books? | He says they are twelve separate books, though often bound together because they are short. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/B-mR9TACPCM?t=13988" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:53:08</a> | What examples of majestic or intensive plural appear in Semitic languages outside the Bible? | He gives a Talmudic example using a plural form for "great king." |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/B-mR9TACPCM?t=14132" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:55:32</a> | Did ancient people understand that languages could be related? | He says they seem to have had some practical sense of language relationships and dialect closeness, even without modern comparative linguistics. |
